core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because the first speaker anchored Apple as the nonconceded focal item, and later agents converged on a top-hold baseline that kept Apple with A1. Agent_5 also secured their top item, so the gap is not pure exclusion; the same norm gave A1 an 86-point item and A5 a 47-point item.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, but with a caveat. The discussion process repeatedly treated Apple-to-Agent_1 as fixed after Agent_1 opened on it, and Round 2 leverage helped make that acceptable. The counterexample is that Agent_5 also got Stone and authored the unanimously accepted final proposal, so the advantage was anchoring plus value asymmetry rather than suppressing the last speaker.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by making Apple top priority and asking for coalition support. Agent_2 immediately accepted Apple-to-A1, and later conflict focused mostly on Pencil rather than Apple. Agent_1's second turn consolidated the final Apple A1, Jewel A2, Pencil A3, Quill A4, Stone A5 baseline.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All five private-thinking entries recognized broad convergence on the same baseline and emphasized preserving top holds. Agent_1 planned to lock the baseline and use Round 2 leverage; other agents treated the baseline as the consensus-friendly path.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Every formal proposal was identical, assigning Apple to A1 and Stone to A5. Proposal reasoning framed the allocation as the consensus/top-priority baseline, turning the proposal stage into ratification.
phase_notes.private_voting: All private votes accepted all five proposals. Voters justified acceptance by securing their own top hold; A1 accepted because Apple delivered 86, while A5 accepted because Stone was secured.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries or reflection section are present in this transcript.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first speaking slot to frame Apple as the core nonconceded issue and invite others to bargain around the remaining items. Agent_2 then treated Apple-to-A1 as the starting premise, so later negotiation had to overcome an established anchor rather than decide Apple from scratch.

evidence.quote: Apple is my top priority (value 86 to me).
If you help me secure Apple

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1's baseline became the group baseline
mechanisms.explanation: After all agents spoke once, Agent_1 restated a complete allocation that preserved Apple and gave others their salient top holds. Subsequent speakers explicitly endorsed it as Agent_1's plan, reducing the later phase to confirmation of that baseline.

mechanisms.theme: Future-round leverage made the anchor acceptable
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's initial compensation frame and later Round 2 language gave others a reason to accept the first-round Apple allocation without reopening Apple. This helped preserve A1's top item while keeping objections focused on future priority or Pencil swaps.

evidence.quote: Round 2 leverage: In exchange for Pencil in Round 1
formalizing a Round 2 advantage for Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Formal phase ratified an already settled allocation
mechanisms.explanation: The formal proposals did not compete over alternative outcomes; all five submitted the same allocation with Apple to A1. The unanimous vote therefore confirmed a public baseline that had already absorbed Agent_1's opening claim.

evidence.quote: consensus Round 1 baseline: Apple to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Top-hold norm and value asymmetry explain the size of the gap
mechanisms.explanation: This is the main counterweight to a pure positional explanation. Agent_5 was not shut out; the last speaker also secured their top item and accepted the same baseline. Agent_1 won by 39 because Apple was worth much more to A1 than Stone was to A5 once both top holds were preserved.
